userNote: STRUCTURAL DEBT: the onFileChange callback at lines 207-419 is the ~215-line inline orchestration that HOOK-ARCHITECTURE-RESEARCH §6 explicitly flagged as the biggest pre-hook procedural seam left in the codebase. HOOKS.FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE and HOOKS.FILE_AFTER_CHANGE are declared in hook-registry.js with documented payloads but have zero publishers. This is what those hooks were defined for. Splitting the add/change/unlink branches into FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE + FILE_AFTER_CHANGE subscribers is the natural next batch — it unlocks per-file plugins (Louis lock checks, real-time UI mirrors, audit listeners) without patching main.js for every new concern.
actionsTaken: Resolved upstream by Wave 2B — see hooks ticket 6. The new onFileChange wrapper is 69 lines (40 LOC of real code) with two FILE_AFTER_CHANGE subscribers (schema-card-emitter P=20, sse-broadcaster P=30). Confirmed via `git log --oneline --grep='decompose 215-line'` → commit 13ec011 'refactor(hooks): decompose 215-line onFileChange into FILE_BEFORE/AFTER hooks'. The onFileChange callback in src/core/server/main.js now fires HOOKS.FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE (line 411) and HOOKS.FILE_AFTER_CHANGE (line 431), with the add/change/unlink dispatch reduced to a thin orchestrator. The named hook constants now have real publishers and Louis lock checks / real-time UI mirrors / audit listeners can subscribe without patching main.js. No Wave 4A code change required.

userNote: REDUNDANT POLL: setInterval at line 199 hits /api/connection-state.json every 5s purely to recolor constellation particles. The same data (mutation events → status changes) already flows through the SSE stream this same file subscribes to at line 903. Subscribe the constellation update to the existing mutationSource.onmessage handler instead and drop the poll. Comment at line 197 even calls this out: 'piggyback by listening on the same window event the toast handler emits, but to keep coupling loose we just poll the manifest periodically too (cheap; 5s).' Cheap, but redundant — and a 5s ceiling on latency for status changes that SSE delivers in <1s.

REDUNDANT POLL: startPolling polls /api/connection-state.json every 2s via setInterval at line 65. The notification bus already exposes the same mutation data over SSE at /api/mutations, AND no current consumer of St8Coordination.addListener exists in src/frontend/ (grep confirms no addListener call sites). Either wire coordination to the SSE stream (cheap, real-time) or delete the service if nothing actually subscribes. Today it does work every 2s that no one reads.

NO TESTS: the debounce-then-flush behavior (_onFileChange + _flush at lines 93-120) is the core of the watcher and has zero unit tests. Edge cases that need coverage: (a) rapid CREATE+EDIT for the same file inside one debounce window — what arrives in the flush array? (b) DELETE arriving during an in-flight flush — does the next debounce see it? (c) callback rejection — does the watcher recover for the next event? (d) pending-changes Set semantics: { path, type } objects are reference-equal not value-equal, so duplicates accumulate. Hand-test today, regression-prone forever without coverage.

BUG-RISK: pendingChanges is `new Set()` but entries are pushed as `{ path, type }` object literals (line 94). Each call creates a fresh object so the Set never deduplicates — a file with 100 'change' events in one debounce window produces 100 entries in the flush. Either switch to a `Map` keyed by `path` (last write wins, deduped) or a `Map<path, Set<type>>` if all event types matter. Today it just inflates the flush array.
actionsTaken: Switched pendingChanges from `new Set()` to `new Map()` keyed by the composite string `${filePath}::${eventType}`. Chose Map+composite-key over Map<path,type> (last-write-wins) because the existing `onFileChange` callback in main.js dispatches by `change.type` — collapsing a CREATE+EDIT for the same path to one EDIT would silently drop the CREATE branch's persistence.upsertFile + initial schema-card emission. The composite key dedupes the storm-pattern (100 'change' events on one file → 1 entry) while preserving CREATE/EDIT/DELETE distinction within a window. Updated _onFileChange to set the key and _flush to iterate `pendingChanges.values()`. Smoke probe: 3 'change' events + 1 'add' on different paths → pendingChanges.size === 2, flush array has 2 entries. Test coverage lands in ticket 3.

userNote: SCHEMA GAP: file_registry.lifecyclePhase is TEXT DEFAULT 'DEVELOPMENT' with no CHECK constraint (line 60). Same for brunoStatus TEXT DEFAULT 'active' (line 70). A typo in _handleProductionPromote ('PORDUCTION') or any future setter lands silently. Add `CHECK (lifecyclePhase IN ('DEVELOPMENT', 'STAGING', 'PRODUCTION'))` and `CHECK (brunoStatus IN ('active', 'flagged', 'archived'))`. Indexes on both columns already exist (idx_file_registry_lifecycle, idx_file_registry_bruno) so the values are already grouped — the constraint just enforces what the queries already assume.

DOUBLE-FIRE INVESTIGATION: when the watcher fires CREATE then EDIT for the same path inside one debounce window, main.js's loop calls publish() twice. Both publishes (a) emit on the EventEmitter (b) broadcast SSE (c) console.log (d) if schemaCard present, call printer.printCard. The printer chain has no idempotency guard — a card with the same fingerprint could be written twice in <1s. Today the schemaCard field is unset by main.js publishers so the printer doesn't actually fire, but the moment any publisher attaches a card the double-fire becomes real. Either dedupe at the watcher (Map by path — see ticket above) or guard the printer write with a recent-fingerprint cache.

VERIFY SSE CLEANUP: addSSEClient at line 96 wires res.on('close') and res.on('error') to delete the response from this.sseClients. Confirmed via read: 'error' handler exists (added explicitly per the inline comment to prevent uncaught process crashes) and 'close' handler exists. One gap: there is no idle-timeout / heartbeat, so a half-open TCP that never fires 'close' or 'error' (e.g. a client whose machine sleeps) will sit in the Set indefinitely, capped at maxSseClients=10. Add a periodic ping (e.g. res.write(': heartbeat\n\n') every 30s) that fails the broken-pipe write loop and naturally drops dead clients via the per-client try/catch in _broadcastSSE.

SSE ROBUSTNESS: /api/mutations endpoint (_handleMutationsSSE at line 755) is a one-liner — it just calls notificationBus.addSSEClient. There is no server-side heartbeat (see notification-bus ticket above), no max-age, and the only test for it is hand-curl. The frontend has reconnect-with-backoff (src/frontend/app.js:945), which masks but does not eliminate dropped connections. Add: (a) a server-side ping every 30s, (b) an integration test that connects, asserts handshake, holds the connection while publish() fires, and verifies the event arrives, (c) a reconnection test that drops mid-stream and verifies frontend recovers.

MISSING AUTOMATION: the file-level docstring claims 'Automatic File Lifecycle Management' and 'Called on every session start' (lines 5, 23) but nothing inside st8 actually invokes runBrunoCall or runOscarHouse on a schedule. They only fire via POST /api/bruno-call and POST /api/oscar-house. Either remove the 'automatic' wording from the docstring OR wire a startup trigger (e.g. a SESSION_START hook subscriber that calls bruno) in src/core/server/main.js after persistence.initialize(). Today the feature is fully manual; the docstring is misleading.

DEAD HOOK SITE: this is the natural firing site for HOOKS.LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION (per batch 025 narrative: 'LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION stays reserved for actual file-phase transitions — bruno+oscar territory'). runOscarHouse changes brunoStatus from 'flagged' to 'archived' and never calls hookRegistry.execute. onEventTriggered changes it back to 'active' and never fires the hook either. Wire HOOKS.LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION with payload { file, oldPhase: 'flagged', newPhase: 'archived' } in runOscarHouse, and the inverse in onEventTriggered. The hook constant exists; the publisher is missing.

DEAD CODE / UNWIRED: _appendToParent (lines 162-182) reads a file, appends its content to the file referenced by associatedWith, and is declared private. Grep across src/ shows zero call sites — runOscarHouse never invokes it. The 'fold stale child into parent before archive' behavior described in the comment is implemented but not connected. Either wire it into runOscarHouse for files with associatedWith set, or remove the method.

SINGLE-TARGET LIMIT: FileWatcher is constructed once at line 209 with the single targetDir from argv. Multi-target setups (e.g. monorepos where the frontend and a sibling backend repo both want indexing) are unsupported. The FileWatcher class itself accepts only one root in its constructor. To extend: either (a) accept multiple --watch args and spin up N watchers, or (b) extend FileWatcher to accept a roots array and configure chokidar with the array form chokidar.watch([...]). Document the limitation in the meantime.

BROAD IGNORES: the ignored-globs list at lines 55-72 includes '**/*.json' and '**/*.toml'. This is to prevent the manifest writer's own output (connection-state.json, ai-signal.toml) from triggering the watcher, but it also ignores user code config files (package.json, tsconfig.json, pyproject.toml). Tighten to specific basenames (connection-state.json, ai-signal.toml) plus the .st8/** glob already present, OR rely solely on the indexer's batch-026 SELF_WRITTEN_BASENAMES guard. Today a package.json change does not trigger re-index.
actionsTaken: Audit verdict: scope DOWN. The broad `**/*.json` + `**/*.toml` ignores were over-scoped after Wave 2B + batch 026: (a) the indexer already has SELF_WRITTEN_BASENAMES = {'connection-state.json', 'ai-signal.toml'} at src/features/indexing/indexer.js:166 that skips those basenames at index time, (b) main.js's CODE_EXTENSIONS filter still includes `.json` (so JSON files are legitimate code targets), (c) the indexer's IGNORE_DIRS already covers .st8/ which holds .json manifests. Replaced `**/*.json` + `**/*.toml` with `path.join(this.targetDir, 'connection-state.json')` + `path.join(this.targetDir, 'ai-signal.toml')` — root-only, exact-path. Now a user `package.json` / `tsconfig.json` / `pyproject.toml` / `Cargo.toml` change correctly flows through the watcher. The other ignores (.sqlite*, node_modules, .git, dist, build, .venv, venv, __pycache__, .st8/**, snapshots/**, .archive/**, .planning/st8_identity_system/**) were left intact — they are scoped to known non-source paths. Inline comment block added above the ignored: list explaining the audit decision and the downstream safety net (CODE_EXTENSIONS + SELF_WRITTEN_BASENAMES).

userNote: NO METRICS: the watcher has no observability — no count of events received, no debounce-merge ratio (input events / flush calls), no last-flush latency. For a long-running --watch session this is the first signal that something is wrong (chokidar event storm, debounce too short, callback blocking too long). Add a lightweight stats object: { eventsReceived, flushes, lastFlushDuration, lastFlushChanges } exposed via a GET /api/watcher/stats endpoint OR logged every N flushes.

STALE COMMENT: header comment (lines 1-10) describes 'multi-LLM manifest synchronization' as the use case — two LLMs reading the same manifest, watcher fires, manifest updates, second LLM sees truth. The actual call site (src/frontend/app.js:363) just starts polling with no addListener consumer anywhere in src/frontend/. Either the multi-LLM design has been quietly deprecated (in which case retire to OGB per the batch-025 state.js precedent), or there's a missing call site to surface listener output. Reconcile the comment with reality.

DORMANT PRINTER CHAIN: setPrinter is called from main.js:92, but the publish() path only invokes printer.printCard when the event carries a schemaCard property — no current publisher attaches that property. main.js emits schema cards out-of-band via emitter.emitCard. Either wire the watcher branches to attach the card (single point of card emission) OR remove the printer hook from publish() and call printer.printCard directly from the emitter path. Today the conditional is structurally dead.
